sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: εἷς ἂν μόνος νιν τῶν ἐμῶν γνοίη φίλων .
sections.translation: There is only one of my friends who would recognize him .

sections.label: Section 286
sections.original: ἆρʼ ὃν λέγουσιν αὐτὸν ἐκκλέψαι φόνου ;
sections.translation: The man who is said to have stolen him away from murder ?

sections.label: Section 287
sections.original: πατρός γε παιδαγωγὸς ἀρχαῖος γέρων .
sections.translation: Yes , the old man , my father ’ s old servant .

sections.label: Section 288
sections.original: ὁ κατθανὼν δὲ σὸς πατὴρ τύμβου κυρεῖ ;
sections.translation: Did the dead man , your father , find burial ?

sections.label: Section 289
sections.original: ἔκυρσεν ὡς ἔκυρσεν , ἐκβληθεὶς δόμων .
sections.translation: He found what he could , cast out of the house .

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: οἴμοι , τόδʼ οἷον εἶπας · — αἴσθησις γὰρ οὖν
sections.translation: Alas , the things you have said ! For perception of suffering , even another ’ s , gnaws at mortals . Speak , so that when I know , I may tell your brother the story , unpleasant , but necessary to hear . Pity is not present at all in clownishness ,

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: σοφοῖσι δʼ ἀνδρῶν · καὶ γὰρ οὐδʼ ἀζήμιον
sections.translation: but in wise men . And indeed it is not without mischief for the wise to have overly profound thoughts .

sections.label: Section 297
sections.original: κἀγὼ τὸν αὐτὸν τῷδʼ ἔρον ψυχῆς ἔχω .
sections.translation: And I have the same desire in my heart as this man . Being far from the town , I do not know the city ’ s scandals , and now I want to learn them .

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: λέγοιμʼ ἄν , εἰ χρή — χρὴ δὲ πρὸς φίλον λέγειν —
sections.translation: I will speak , if I must — and one must speak to a friend — about my own and my father ’ s heavy misfortunes . Since you are setting the tale in motion , I entreat you , stranger , tell Orestes of our sorrows , mine and his . First of all , in what clothes I live like a beast in a stall ,

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: πίνῳ θʼ ὅσῳ βέβριθʼ , ὑπὸ στέγαισί τε
sections.translation: with what filth I am weighted down , under what roof I dwell , having lived in a royal home ; I myself working hard on my clothes at the loom , or else I shall go barely clad and do without ; always carrying water from the springs myself ,

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: ἀνέορτος ἱερῶν καὶ χορῶν τητωμένη .
sections.translation: with no share in the festival rites , no part in the dance . I turn away from married women , as a virgin ; and I turn away from Castor , who sought me in marriage before he joined the gods , for I was his relative . But my mother , in the spoils of Troy ,

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: θρόνῳ κάθηται , πρὸς δʼ ἕδραισιν Ἀσίδες
sections.translation: is seated on her throne , and at her chair stand slaves from Asia , my father ’ s plunder , fastening their Trojan robes with golden brooches . And still my father ’ s blood has rotted black on the wall , while the one who killed him

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: ἐς ταὐτὰ βαίνων ἅρματʼ ἐκφοιτᾷ πατρί ,
sections.translation: mounts the same chariot and goes forth ; and is proud to hold in his blood-stained hands the scepter with which my father used to command the Hellenes . Agamemnon ’ s grave , dishonoured , has not yet ever received any libations , or branch of myrtle ,

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: ἔλαβε , πυρὰ δὲ χέρσος ἀγλαϊσμάτων .
sections.translation: but his altar is barren of ornament . That famous one , my mother ’ s husband , leaps on the grave , they say , when soaked in drink , and pelts my father ’ s marble monument with stones , and dares to say this to us :

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: ποῦ παῖς Ὀρέστης ; ἆρά σοι τύμβῳ καλῶς
sections.translation: Where is your son Orestes ? Is he here to defend the tomb for you nobly ? Orestes is insulted in this way while absent . But , stranger , I beg you , report these things . There are many calling him to come — I am their interpreter — these hands , this tongue , my broken heart ,

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: κάρα τʼ ἐμὸν ξυρῆκες , ὅ τʼ ἐκεῖνον τεκών .
sections.translation: my shorn head , and his own father . For it is shameful , if his father exterminated the Trojans but Orestes is unable to kill a man , one against one , being young and born from a more noble father .

sections.label: Section 339
sections.original: καὶ μὴν δέδορκα τόνδε , σὸν λέγω πόσιν ,
sections.translation: And look , I see him , I mean your husband ,

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: λήξαντα μόχθου πρὸς δόμους ὡρμημένον .
sections.translation: on his way home , his day ’ s work done .

sections.label: Section 341
sections.original: ἔα · τίνας τούσδʼ ἐν πύλαις ὁρῶ ξένους ;
sections.translation: Oh ! who are these strangers I see at my door ? Why have they come here to my rustic gate ? Do they want something from me ? For it is shameful for a woman to be standing with young men .

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: ὦ φίλτατʼ , εἰς ὕποπτα μὴ μόλῃς ἐμοί ·
sections.translation: Dearest , do not be suspicious of me ; you shall hear the truth ; for these strangers have come to me as messengers of news from Orestes . But , strangers , pardon him for what he said .

sections.label: Section 349
sections.original: τί φασίν ; ἁνὴρ ἔστι καὶ λεύσσει φάος ;
sections.translation: What do they say ? The man is alive and sees the light of day ?

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: ἔστιν λόγῳ γοῦν , φασὶ δʼ οὐκ ἄπιστʼ ἐμοί .
sections.translation: He is , at least in their report , and I believe them .

sections.label: Section 351
sections.original: ἦ καί τι πατρὸς σῶν τε μέμνηται κακῶν ;
sections.translation: Surely then he remembers something of your father ’ s wrongs and your own ?

sections.label: Section 352
sections.original: ἐν ἐλπίσιν ταῦτʼ · ἀσθενὴς φεύγων ἀνήρ .
sections.translation: These are things to hope for ; a man in exile is powerless .

sections.label: Section 353
sections.original: ἦλθον δʼ Ὀρέστου τίνʼ ἀγορεύοντες λόγον ;
sections.translation: What message from Orestes have they come to declare ?

sections.label: Section 354
sections.original: σκοποὺς ἔπεμψε τούσδε τῶν ἐμῶν κακῶν .
sections.translation: He sent them as spies of my wrongs .

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: οὐκοῦν τὰ μὲν λεύσσουσι , τὰ δὲ σύ που λέγεις .
sections.translation: Then they see a part of it , and perhaps you are telling them the rest .

sections.label: Section 356
sections.original: ἴσασιν , οὐδὲν τῶνδʼ ἔχουσιν ἐνδεές .
sections.translation: They know ; they have all these things in abundance .

sections.label: Section 357
sections.original: οὐκοῦν πάλαι χρῆν τοῖσδʼ ἀνεπτύχθαι πύλας ;
sections.translation: Then shouldn ’ t you have opened the doors to them long before ? Go into the house ; for in return for your good tidings , you shall find as much hospitality as my house holds in store .

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: αἴρεσθʼ , ὀπαδοί , τῶνδʼ ἔσω τεύχη δόμων .
sections.translation: Servants , take their baggage within the house . Do not contradict me , since you are friends coming from a friend ; for , even if I am poor , I will not display manners that are ill-bred .

sections.label: Section 364
sections.original: πρὸς θεῶν , ὅδʼ ἁνὴρ ὃς συνεκκλέπτει γάμους
sections.translation: By the gods ! Is this the man who makes a fraud of your marriage ,

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: τοὺς σούς , Ὀρέστην οὐ καταισχύνειν θέλων ;
sections.translation: because he does not want to shame Orestes ?

sections.label: Section 366
sections.original: οὗτος κέκληται πόσις ἐμὸς τῆς ἀθλίας .
sections.translation: This is the one who is called my husband , unhappy as I am .

sections.label: Section 367
sections.original: φεῦ ·
sections.translation: Ah ! There is no exact way to test a man ’ s worth ; for human nature has confusion in it . For instance , I have seen before now the son of a noble father

sections.label: Section 370
sections.original: τὸ μηδὲν ὄντα , χρηστά τʼ ἐκ κακῶν τέκνα ,
sections.translation: worth nothing , and good children from evil parents ; famine in a rich man ’ s spirit , and a mighty soul in a poor man ’ s body . How then does one rightly distinguish and judge these things ? By wealth ? A sorry test to use .

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: ἢ τοῖς ἔχουσι μηδέν ; ἀλλʼ ἔχει νόσον
sections.translation: Or by those who have nothing ? But poverty has a disease , it teaches a man to be wicked in his need . But shall I turn to warfare ? Who , facing the enemy ’ s spear , could be a witness as to who is brave ? It is best to leave these matters alone , at random .

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: οὗτος γὰρ ἁνὴρ οὔτʼ ἐν Ἀργείοις μέγας
sections.translation: For this man , neither important in Argos , nor puffed up by the good reputation of his family , but one of the many , has been found to be the best . Do not be foolish , you who wander about full of empty notions , but judge those noble among men by their company

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: κρινεῖτε καὶ τοῖς ἤθεσιν τοὺς εὐγενεῖς ;
sections.translation: and by their habits . For such men rule well both states and homes ; while those bodies that are empty of mind are only ornaments in the market-place . For the strong arm does not await the battle any better than the weak ;

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: ἐν τῇ φύσει δὲ τοῦτο κἀν εὐψυχίᾳ .
sections.translation: this depends on natural courage . But , since Agamemnon ’ s son , both present and not present , for whose sake we have come , is worthy of it , let us accept a lodging in this house . Calling to his servants . We must go within this house , slaves . May a man poor

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: εἴη πρόθυμος πλουσίου μᾶλλον ξένος .
sections.translation: but eager be a better host for me than a rich man ! And so I am content with the reception into this man ’ s house , though I would have wanted your brother , in good fortune , to lead me to his fortunate home . Perhaps he may come ; the oracles of Loxias are

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: χρησμοί , βροτῶν δὲ μαντικὴν χαίρειν ἐῶ .
sections.translation: sure , but human prophecy I dismiss . Exeunt Orestes and Pylades .

sections.label: Section 401
sections.original: νῦν ἢ πάροιθεν μᾶλλον , Ἠλέκτρα , χαρᾷ
sections.translation: Now more than before , Electra , I feel the warmth of joy at my heart ; for perhaps good fortune , advancing with difficulty , might come to a good resting-place .

sections.label: Section 404
sections.original: ὦ τλῆμον , εἰδὼς δωμάτων χρείαν σέθεν
sections.translation: O reckless man , why , knowing the poverty of your house ,

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: τί τούσδʼ ἐδέξω μείζονας σαυτοῦ ξένους ;
sections.translation: did you welcome these strangers , greater than you ?

sections.label: Section 406
sections.original: τί δʼ ; εἴπερ εἰσὶν ὡς δοκοῦσιν εὐγενεῖς ,
sections.translation: What ? If they are really as noble as they seem , won ’ t they be equally content among great and small ?

sections.label: Section 408
sections.original: ἐπεί νυν ἐξήμαρτες ἐν σμικροῖσιν ὤν ,
sections.translation: Since you , one of the small , have now made this error , go to my father ’ s dear old servant ,

sections.label: Section 410
sections.original: ὃς ἀμφὶ ποταμὸν Τάναον Ἀργείας ὅρους
sections.translation: who tends his flocks , an outcast from the city , by the river Tanaus which cuts a boundary between Argive land and the land of Sparta ; bid him come , since these men have arrived at my house , and provide something for the guests ’ meal .

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: ἡσθήσεταί τοι καὶ προσεύξεται θεοῖς ,
sections.translation: He will be glad , and will offer prayers to the gods , when he hears that the child , whom he once saved , is alive . I cannot get anything from my mother or from my father ’ s house ; for we would bring bitter news , if she , the hard-hearted , were to learn that Orestes is still alive .

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: ἀλλʼ , εἰ δοκεῖ σοι , τούσδʼ ἀπαγγελῶ λόγους
sections.translation: I will take this message to the old man , if you wish ; but go inside the house at once and make things ready there . Surely a woman , if she wants to , can find many additions to a meal . Really there is still enough in the house

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: ὥσθʼ ἕν γʼ ἐπʼ ἦμαρ τούσδε πληρῶσαι βορᾶς .
sections.translation: to cram them with food for one day at least . It is in such cases , whenever I fail in my intentions , that I see how wealth has great power , to give to strangers , and to expend in curing the body when it falls sick ; but money for our daily food

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: ἐς σμικρὸν ἥκει · πᾶς γὰρ ἐμπλησθεὶς ἀνὴρ
sections.translation: comes to little ; for every man when full , rich or poor , gets an equal amount . Exeunt Electra and Peasant .

sections.label: Section 1.432
sections.original: κλειναὶ νᾶες , αἵ ποτʼ ἔβατε Τροίαν
sections.translation: O famous ships , you that once with countless oars went to Troy , conducting dances with the Nereids ,

sections.label: Section 1.435
sections.original: ἵνʼ ὁ φίλαυλος ἔπαλλε δελ-
sections.translation: where the music-loving dolphin leapt and rolled at your dark-blue prows , bringing Achilles , the son of Thetis , light in the leap of his foot ,

sections.label: Section 1.440
sections.original: σὺν Ἀγαμέμνονι Τρωίας
sections.translation: with Agamemnon to the banks of Trojan Simois .

sections.label: Section 1.442
sections.original: Νηρῇδες δʼ Εὐβοῖδας ἄκρας λιποῦσαι
sections.translation: The Nereids , leaving Euboea ’ s headlands , brought from Hephaestus ’ anvil his shield-work of golden armor ,

sections.label: Section 1.445
sections.original: ἀνά τε Πήλιον ἀνά τε πρυ-
sections.translation: up to Pelion and the glens at the foot of holy Ossa , the Nymphs ’ watch-tower . . . where his father , the horseman , was training the son of Thetis as a light for Hellas ,

sections.label: Section 1.450
sections.original: Θέτιδος εἰνάλιον γόνον
sections.translation: sea-born , swift-footed for the sons of Atreus .

sections.label: Section 2.452
sections.original: Ἰλιόθεν δʼ ἔκλυόν τινος ἐν λιμέσιν
sections.translation: I heard , from someone who had arrived at the harbor of Nauplia from Ilium , that

sections.label: Section 2.455
sections.original: κλεινᾶς ἀσπίδος ἐν κύκλῳ
sections.translation: on the circle of your famous shield , O son of Thetis , were wrought these signs , a terror to the Phrygians : on the surrounding base of the shield ’ s rim , Perseus the throat-cutter , over

sections.label: Section 2.460
sections.original: ἁλὸς ποτανοῖσι πεδί-
sections.translation: the sea with winged sandals , was holding the Gorgon ’ s body , with Hermes , Zeus ’ messenger , the rustic son of Maia .
